input.goal: Summarize the proposed fix for the false-positive keybinding overlap warning, including root cause, affected areas/files mentioned, and the recommended implementation approach.

content.text:
The proposed fix was:

- Root cause:
  - The extension conflict warning was built from the full builtin keybinding set.
  - That included bindings that are not active in the normal editor context.
  - Because of that, extensions could get warned about overlaps with keys that only apply in special UI states, which produced false positives.

- Main idea:
  - Restrict conflict detection to only the actions that are actually active while the user is typing in the main editor/input context.

- Recommended approach:
  - Add a static allowlist such as `EDITOR_CONTEXT_ACTIONS` or `MAIN_INPUT_ACTIONS` in `runner.ts`.
  - This allowlist should include:
    - all `AppAction` values
    - only the `EditorAction` values that are truly active during normal editor input, such as cursor movement, deletion, clipboard, text entry, tool expand, undo, and kill ring actions

- Explicitly exclude:
  - Session-selector-only actions:
    - `toggleSessionPath`
    - `toggleSessionSort`
    - `renameSession`
    - `deleteSession`
    - `deleteSessionNoninvasive`
  - Modal or selection-only actions:
    - `selectUp`
    - `selectDown`
    - `selectPageUp`
    - `selectPageDown`
    - `selectConfirm`
    - `selectCancel`

- Implementation detail:
  - Update `buildBuiltinKeybindings()` or add a filtering step so conflict detection only considers actions from that allowlist.

- Why this approach:
  - It is simple and predictable.
  - It avoids trying to model every runtime UI state.
  - It matches the real question the warning should answer: “Will this extension shortcut conflict during normal input handling?”

The session described this as the preferred fix, effectively “Option A: static editor-context action set.”

input.task: Propose scalable alternatives to a static allowlist for keybinding conflict detection false positives. Focus on designs that prevent implementors from forgetting to classify new native keybindings when they are added.
input.context: In pi-mono, there is a false-positive extension keybinding overlap warning because conflict detection considers builtin keybindings that are not active in the normal editor context. A previous session recommended a static allowlist of actions active in main input. The user now wants alternative implementations besides an allowlist, especially approaches that scale as more native keybindings are added and reduce the chance that implementors forget to mark whether new keybindings should participate in conflict detection.

content.text:
Yes. The scalable alternatives all have the same theme: do not maintain a separate list for conflict checking. Instead, make conflict eligibility come from the same metadata that defines when a keybinding is active.

Best alternatives, from strongest to weakest:

1. Required context/scope on every native keybinding
- Add a required field like `context` or `scope` to every builtin keybinding definition.
- Examples:
  - `editorTextInput`
  - `editorNormal`
  - `sessionList`
  - `commandPalette`
  - `global`
  - `modal:*`
- Then conflict detection only checks bindings whose context overlaps the extension context.

Why this scales:
- Adding a new native keybinding forces the implementor to classify it.
- There is no second allowlist to forget updating.
- TypeScript can make missing context a compile error.

This is probably the best option.

2. Required `when` predicate as the source of truth
- If the system already has activation predicates, make every native keybinding declare a required `when`.
- Examples:
  - `editorFocused && !sessionSelectorOpen`
  - `sessionSelectorOpen`
  - `autocompleteOpen`
- Conflict detection then asks: can the extension binding and native binding both be active in the same state?

Why this scales:
- It is the most correct model.
- Runtime behavior and conflict detection use the same data.

Trade-off:
- More implementation work.
- You need either a simplified overlap checker or a constrained expression model.

3. Single registration helper that requires classification
- Route all builtin keybindings through one helper like:
  - `registerNativeKeybinding({ action, keys, context })`
- Do not allow ad hoc keybinding objects scattered across files.
- The helper enforces required metadata and can run validation.

Why this scales:
- It prevents new bindings from bypassing the classification system.
- Easy place to add dev warnings and tests.

This works best together with option 1 or 2.

4. Action metadata instead of keybinding allowlist
- Put metadata on the action definition itself:
  - `conflictScope: "editor" | "modal" | "global" | ...`
  - or `contexts: [...]`
- Then all keybindings attached to that action inherit the classification.

Why this scales:
- If actions are the real stable abstraction, this may be cleaner than classifying each binding instance.
- New actions must declare their scope.

Trade-off:
- Less precise if one action can be bound differently in different contexts.

5. Explicit opt-in/opt-out boolean, but enforced
- Add a required field like `participatesInExtensionConflictCheck: boolean`.
- No default allowed.
- CI fails if any builtin keybinding lacks it.

Why this scales better than an allowlist:
- New bindings must declare intent.

Why it is weaker:
- It creates a second truth separate from actual runtime context.
- People can still set the wrong boolean.

So I would only do this if adding real context metadata is too invasive.

What I would recommend

If you want scalable and hard-to-forget:
- Preferred: required `context` on every native keybinding definition
- Better if already supported: required `when` predicate reused for runtime and conflict detection
- Also do: central registration helper + CI validation

That gives you:
- compile-time enforcement
- one source of truth
- less drift over time

Concrete guardrails to prevent forgetting

Whatever design you choose, add these:
- No default for `context` or `when`
- One registration function for all builtin keybindings
- A test that enumerates all builtin keybindings and fails if any are unclassified
- Optional snapshot test so new bindings show up clearly in PR review

What I would avoid
- A permanent manual allowlist in conflict detection
- A parallel “reserved for conflict checking” registry
- Inferring behavior from action names or file locations

Short version:
The best non-allowlist design is to make every native keybinding declare where it is active, and derive conflict checking from that. That is the cleanest way to scale and the best way to make forgetting difficult.

If you want, I can now look at the current keybinding data structures in `pi-mono` and tell you which of these designs fits the existing code best.

content.text:
Yes. The scope idea fits this well.

I would model it like this:

- every native keybinding gets a required `scope`
- conflict detection only considers scopes where extension shortcuts can actually fire
- “reserved vs overridable” stays as a separate concern

That keeps the design clean:
- `scope` answers: when is this binding active?
- `overridePolicy` answers: if an extension overlaps, is that forbidden or just warned?

A concrete sketch

```ts
type KeybindingScope =
	| "global"
	| "editor"
	| "selection"
	| "sessionPicker"
	| "treePicker";

type OverridePolicy = "reserved" | "warn" | "allow";

interface NativeKeybindingDef<TAction extends string> {
	action: TAction;
	keys: KeyId | KeyId[];
	scope: KeybindingScope;
	overridePolicy: OverridePolicy;
}
```

Then you stop deriving builtin conflict keys from a flat merged map and instead define native bindings with metadata:

```ts
const NATIVE_KEYBINDINGS: NativeKeybindingDef<KeyAction>[] = [
	{ action: "interrupt", keys: "escape", scope: "global", overridePolicy: "reserved" },
	{ action: "clear", keys: "ctrl+c", scope: "editor", overridePolicy: "reserved" },
	{ action: "exit", keys: "ctrl+d", scope: "editor", overridePolicy: "reserved" },

	{ action: "cursorLeft", keys: ["left", "ctrl+b"], scope: "editor", overridePolicy: "warn" },
	{ action: "submit", keys: "enter", scope: "editor", overridePolicy: "reserved" },

	{ action: "selectUp", keys: "up", scope: "selection", overridePolicy: "warn" },
	{ action: "selectConfirm", keys: "enter", scope: "selection", overridePolicy: "reserved" },

	{ action: "toggleSessionPath", keys: "ctrl+p", scope: "sessionPicker", overridePolicy: "warn" },
	{ action: "renameSession", keys: "ctrl+r", scope: "sessionPicker", overridePolicy: "warn" },

	{ action: "treeFoldOrUp", keys: ["ctrl+left", "alt+left"], scope: "treePicker", overridePolicy: "warn" },
];
```

Then conflict detection becomes something like:

```ts
const EXTENSION_SHORTCUT_SCOPES: ReadonlySet<KeybindingScope> = new Set([
	"global",
	"editor",
]);

function participatesInExtensionConflictCheck(binding: NativeKeybindingDef<KeyAction>): boolean {
	return EXTENSION_SHORTCUT_SCOPES.has(binding.scope);
}
```

And:

```ts
function buildBuiltinConflictMap(bindings: NativeKeybindingDef<KeyAction>[]) {
	const result = new Map<KeyId, { action: KeyAction; overridePolicy: OverridePolicy; scope: KeybindingScope }>();

	for (const binding of bindings) {
		if (!participatesInExtensionConflictCheck(binding)) continue;

		const keys = Array.isArray(binding.keys) ? binding.keys : [binding.keys];
		for (const key of keys) {
			result.set(key.toLowerCase() as KeyId, {
				action: binding.action,
				overridePolicy: binding.overridePolicy,
				scope: binding.scope,
			});
		}
	}

	return result;
}
```

How many scopes do we need from the current keymaps?

Based on the existing actions, I think you only need 5 scopes right now.

1. `global`
Active regardless of editor text state, in the main interactive UI.

Native actions that fit:
- `interrupt`
- possibly `suspend`
- maybe `cycleThinkingLevel`
- maybe `toggleThinking`
- maybe `expandTools`
- maybe `externalEditor`
- maybe `followUp`

I say “maybe” because some of these are conceptually main-editor actions, but from a conflict perspective they behave like always-available app shortcuts while you are in the normal interactive view.

2. `editor`
Normal main editor/input context.

This includes:
- cursor movement
- deletion
- text input
- clipboard
- kill ring
- undo
- app actions tied to the main input view

Concretely, likely:
- `cursorUp`
- `cursorDown`
- `cursorLeft`
- `cursorRight`
- `cursorWordLeft`
- `cursorWordRight`
- `cursorLineStart`
- `cursorLineEnd`
- `jumpForward`
- `jumpBackward`
- `pageUp`
- `pageDown`
- `deleteCharBackward`
- `deleteCharForward`
- `deleteWordBackward`
- `deleteWordForward`
- `deleteToLineStart`
- `deleteToLineEnd`
- `newLine`
- `submit`
- `tab`
- `copy`
- `yank`
- `yankPop`
- `undo`
- `pasteImage`
- probably `clear`
- probably `exit`
- probably `selectModel`
- probably `cycleModelForward`
- probably `cycleModelBackward`
- probably `toggleThinking`
- probably `expandTools`
- probably `externalEditor`
- probably `followUp`
- probably `dequeue`

This is the main scope that should be checked for conflicts.

3. `selection`
Generic picker/list/autocomplete selection mode.

This includes:
- `selectUp`
- `selectDown`
- `selectPageUp`
- `selectPageDown`
- `selectConfirm`
- `selectCancel`

This scope covers autocomplete, model selector, config selector, extension selector, dialogs, and other list-style UI.

These should not be checked for extension shortcut conflicts if extension shortcuts do not fire while selectors are active.

4. `sessionPicker`
Session-selector-specific actions.

This includes:
- `toggleSessionPath`
- `toggleSessionSort`
- `toggleSessionNamedFilter`
- `renameSession`
- `deleteSession`
- `deleteSessionNoninvasive`

These definitely should not be checked, based on the false positive you found.

One subtle point:
- `toggleSessionNamedFilter` is currently an `AppAction`, not an `EditorAction`, but conceptually it belongs in `sessionPicker` scope, not `global`.

That is actually a good example of why scope is better than relying on action type.

5. `treePicker`
Tree-selector-specific navigation.

This includes:
- `treeFoldOrUp`
- `treeUnfoldOrDown`
- probably also `tree` itself is not this scope; `tree` is more like a command/open-action with no default key

These should also not be checked if extension shortcuts do not fire in the tree modal.

Which scopes should be checked for extension conflicts?

Based on the earlier session and the current behavior, I would check only:

- `global`
- `editor`

And I would not check:
- `selection`
- `sessionPicker`
- `treePicker`

That matches the principle:
only warn when the native keybinding and extension shortcut can both be active in the same input context.

So the rule is simple:

```ts
const CONFLICT_CHECKED_SCOPES = new Set<KeybindingScope>([
	"global",
	"editor",
]);
```

How this helps implementors not forget

This is the main benefit.

If you make `scope` required everywhere:
- every new native keybinding must declare a scope
- the code compiling is what forces the decision
- the conflict checker automatically includes or excludes the new binding based on that scope
- nobody has to remember to update a second allowlist

That is the real win.

What I would do structurally

I would go one step further and stop treating defaults as just:

```ts
Record<Action, KeyId | KeyId[]>
```

for internal/native definitions.

Instead, keep a richer native definition:

```ts
type NativeBindingsByAction<TAction extends string> = Record<
	TAction,
	{
		keys: KeyId | KeyId[];
		scope: KeybindingScope;
		overridePolicy: OverridePolicy;
	}
>;
```

Example:

```ts
export const DEFAULT_EDITOR_KEYBINDINGS: NativeBindingsByAction<EditorAction> = {
	cursorUp: { keys: "up", scope: "editor", overridePolicy: "warn" },
	selectUp: { keys: "up", scope: "selection", overridePolicy: "warn" },
	toggleSessionPath: { keys: "ctrl+p", scope: "sessionPicker", overridePolicy: "warn" },
	submit: { keys: "enter", scope: "editor", overridePolicy: "reserved" },
	selectConfirm: { keys: "enter", scope: "selection", overridePolicy: "reserved" },
};
```

Then expose a flattened helper where the rest of the app still needs plain keys:

```ts
function getDefaultKeyMap(defs: NativeBindingsByAction<KeyAction>): Record<KeyAction, KeyId | KeyId[]> {
	// return just keys
}
```

That lets you add scope without forcing the whole codebase to switch all at once.

My recommended scope map for the current code

- `global`
  - app-wide shortcuts active in the normal interactive screen
- `editor`
  - normal text editor/input shortcuts
- `selection`
  - generic lists, pickers, autocomplete, dialog selection
- `sessionPicker`
  - session-selector-only actions
- `treePicker`
  - tree navigator specific actions

That is enough for the current keymaps.

If you wanted even less granularity, you could merge `sessionPicker` and `treePicker` into `modal`.
Then you would have only 4 scopes:
- `global`
- `editor`
- `selection`
- `modal`

But I think 5 is better because:
- it matches the current action groups
- it leaves room for clearer diagnostics
- it avoids stuffing unrelated modal actions into one bucket too early

Example diagnostic quality

With scopes, warnings can become more precise:

- forbidden:
  - `Extension shortcut 'ctrl+c' conflicts with reserved built-in shortcut 'clear' in scope 'editor'. Skipping.`
- warning:
  - `Extension shortcut 'ctrl+b' overrides built-in shortcut 'cursorLeft' in scope 'editor'.`
- no warning:
  - no conflict emitted for `toggleSessionPath` because it is in `sessionPicker` scope

My recommendation

If you implement this, I would do:

- 5 scopes:
  - `global`
  - `editor`
  - `selection`
  - `sessionPicker`
  - `treePicker`
- conflict-check only:
  - `global`
  - `editor`
- keep `overridePolicy` separate from `scope`
- make `scope` required in the native keybinding definition type
- add a test that every native binding has a valid scope and that conflict filtering only includes `global` and `editor`

If you want, I can next sketch how this would map onto the current files:
- `packages/tui/src/keybindings.ts`
- `packages/coding-agent/src/core/keybindings.ts`
- `packages/coding-agent/src/core/extensions/runner.ts`

with a migration path that does not require rewriting everything at once.

messages.content: why is the override policy? are we currently preventing some keybinds from being overriden? if it's not the case skip that. let's keep things simple and maintainable

content.text:
Yes, there is currently an override policy already, just not named that way.

Today it is this in:

- `packages/coding-agent/src/core/extensions/runner.ts`

Specifically:
- `RESERVED_ACTIONS_FOR_EXTENSION_CONFLICTS`

That list marks built-in actions whose keybindings extensions cannot override. The current behavior is:

- if a built-in key is for a reserved action:
  - extension shortcut is skipped
- if a built-in key is for a non-reserved action:
  - extension shortcut is allowed, but a warning is emitted
- if two extensions use the same key:
  - later one wins, with a warning

So yes, some keybinds are currently prevented from being overridden by extensions.

That said, if your goal here is only to fix the false positives and keep the design simple, then I agree: do not introduce a new “override policy” abstraction as part of this change.

Keep it simple

I would keep the existing reserved-action mechanism exactly as it is, and only add scope.

So the model becomes:

```ts
type KeybindingScope =
	| "global"
	| "editor"
	| "selection"
	| "sessionPicker"
	| "treePicker";
```

And each native keybinding just gets:

```ts
interface NativeKeybindingDef<TAction extends string> {
	action: TAction;
	keys: KeyId | KeyId[];
	scope: KeybindingScope;
}
```

Then conflict detection does two steps:

1. Filter built-in keybindings by scope
- only include scopes that can actually conflict with extension shortcuts

2. Apply the existing reserved-action check
- if action is in `RESERVED_ACTIONS_FOR_EXTENSION_CONFLICTS`, skip extension shortcut
- otherwise warn and allow override

That keeps responsibilities separate without adding new concepts.

What the simple version could look like

```ts
type KeybindingScope =
	| "global"
	| "editor"
	| "selection"
	| "sessionPicker"
	| "treePicker";

interface BuiltinKeybindingEntry {
	action: KeyAction;
	keys: KeyId | KeyId[];
	scope: KeybindingScope;
}
```

Then:

```ts
const CONFLICT_CHECKED_SCOPES = new Set<KeybindingScope>(["global", "editor"]);
```

And:

```ts
function participatesInExtensionConflictCheck(scope: KeybindingScope): boolean {
	return CONFLICT_CHECKED_SCOPES.has(scope);
}
```

Then in the runner:
- build the builtin conflict map from only `global` and `editor`
- keep `RESERVED_ACTIONS_FOR_EXTENSION_CONFLICTS` as-is

How many scopes do we need?

I would still suggest 5 from the current keymaps:

- `global`
- `editor`
- `selection`
- `sessionPicker`
- `treePicker`

Why these 5:
- they map cleanly to the existing action groups
- they are easy to understand
- they avoid one vague catch-all “modal” bucket

Which should be checked for conflicts?

Only:
- `global`
- `editor`

Not checked:
- `selection`
- `sessionPicker`
- `treePicker`

How current actions likely map

`global`
- `interrupt`
- maybe a few app-level shortcuts that are available in the main interactive screen regardless of text editing details

`editor`
- cursor movement
- deletion
- text input
- clipboard
- kill ring
- undo
- normal main-input app shortcuts like model cycling, tool expand, thinking toggle, external editor, follow-up, dequeue, paste image

`selection`
- `selectUp`
- `selectDown`
- `selectPageUp`
- `selectPageDown`
- `selectConfirm`
- `selectCancel`

`sessionPicker`
- `toggleSessionPath`
- `toggleSessionSort`
- `toggleSessionNamedFilter`
- `renameSession`
- `deleteSession`
- `deleteSessionNoninvasive`

`treePicker`
- `treeFoldOrUp`
- `treeUnfoldOrDown`

One important note:
- `toggleSessionNamedFilter` is currently an `AppAction`, but with scopes that does not matter. It can still correctly be marked `sessionPicker`.

That is another benefit of the scope design: it classifies by activation context, not by which TypeScript union the action lives in.

Recommended minimal design

I would propose exactly this:

1. Add `KeybindingScope`
2. Add a native keybinding definition structure with `action`, `keys`, `scope`
3. Annotate all built-in keybindings with a scope
4. In extension conflict detection, only include `global` and `editor`
5. Keep `RESERVED_ACTIONS_FOR_EXTENSION_CONFLICTS` unchanged

No extra override abstraction.
No fancy predicate system.
Just one new required field that becomes the source of truth for conflict eligibility.

That is the simplest maintainable version.

messages.content: are keybinds always mapped to a single scope? is there a binding/key that is used for two different actions in different scopes?

content.thinking:
**Explaining key bindings**

I need to clarify that the same key can trigger multiple actions in different contexts. For instance, the Ctrl+P could cycle the model forward in one scenario and toggle the session path in another, with other keys like up, down, or enter performing distinct functions as well. What’s important to note is the distinction in bindings: each action currently belongs to a single scope, even if the same physical key appears in multiple bindings across various scopes.

content.text:
No, not globally.

Two different things are true now:

- each native action binding entry has exactly one scope
- the same physical key can absolutely be reused by different actions in different scopes

So the model is:

- `submit` has one scope: `editor`
- `selectConfirm` has one scope: `selection`
- both can use `enter`

Examples already in the current keymaps

Same key, different actions, different scopes:

- `enter`
  - `submit` in `editor`
  - `selectConfirm` in `selection`

- `up`
  - `cursorUp` in `editor`
  - `selectUp` in `selection`

- `down`
  - `cursorDown` in `editor`
  - `selectDown` in `selection`

- `pageUp`
  - `pageUp` in `editor`
  - `selectPageUp` in `selection`

- `pageDown`
  - `pageDown` in `editor`
  - `selectPageDown` in `selection`

- `ctrl+c`
  - `clear` in `editor`
  - `selectCancel` in `selection`
  - `copy` in `editor`

- `ctrl+d`
  - `exit` in `editor`
  - `deleteCharForward` in `editor`
  - `deleteSession` in `sessionPicker`

- `ctrl+p`
  - `cycleModelForward` in `editor`
  - `toggleSessionPath` in `sessionPicker`

- `ctrl+o`
  - `expandTools` in `editor` as an app action
  - `expandTools` in `editor` as an editor action too

That last one is same scope, same key, same semantic action name, just defined from both sides today.

So to your question directly

- Is a keybinding always mapped to a single scope?
  - No, a key is not.
  - A binding entry is.

- Is there a key used for two different actions in different scopes?
  - Yes, many.
  - That is normal and expected.

Why this scope model still works

Because conflict detection should not ask:
- “is this key used anywhere?”

It should ask:
- “is this key used in a scope where the extension shortcut can also be active?”

That is why `ctrl+p` for `toggleSessionPath` should not warn if extensions only conflict with `editor` and `global` scopes.

One subtle limitation in the current first pass

Right now, each action has one scope for all of its keys. That is fine for the current keymaps, but it assumes:
- all keys for a given action are active in the same UI context

That is true for the current built-ins as far as this first pass goes.

If later you ever need something like:
- one key for action X in `editor`
- another key for the same action X in `global`

then the metadata should move from:
- action -> `{ keys, scope }`

to:
- array of entries -> `{ action, keys, scope }`

or even:
- action -> `[{ key, scope }, ...]`

But I would not do that yet. The current model is simpler and fits the existing definitions well enough.

So short answer:
- one action binding entry currently has one scope
- one physical key can be reused across many scopes
- yes, the existing keymaps already do that in several places

content.text:
Reviewer feedback:

- Summary
  - Change looks good overall
  - low risk
  - main concern: silent collisions while building builtin conflict key maps

Findings

- P1: silent last-write-wins collision in `buildBuiltinKeybindings`
  - file: `packages/coding-agent/src/core/extensions/runner.ts`
  - `expandTools` exists in both editor and app metadata with `ctrl+o`
  - current result is harmless because both entries agree
  - but future duplicate keys across the two maps could silently overwrite each other

- P2: fragile `configuredKeys` lookup
  - file: `packages/coding-agent/src/core/extensions/runner.ts`
  - if `effectiveKeybindings[action]` were ever `undefined`, code could eventually hit `undefined.toLowerCase()`
  - today types protect it, but a guard would make it safer

- P2: `expandTools` duplicated across metadata maps
  - files:
    - `packages/tui/src/keybindings.ts`
    - `packages/coding-agent/src/core/keybindings.ts`
  - current patch makes this duplication more important because conflict detection now consumes both metadata maps directly

- P3: `KeybindingScope` lives in `pi-tui`, used from `coding-agent`
  - probably fine
  - reviewer suggests a comment for future maintainers

Verdict
- patch is correct for current inputs
- best follow-up would be to address the silent collision issue

My take
- I agree with the P1 finding
- the most practical next fix is:
  - add a duplicate-key guard in `buildBuiltinKeybindings`
  - likely ignore exact duplicates when action/scope/restrictOverride match
  - warn or throw if they differ

If you want, I can implement that next.

messages.content: why would we need a duplicate key guard there? how can it be silent, shouldn't that work still with our changes? how does it work in the main branch ?

content.thinking:
**Explaining branch behavior**

I need to explain the current behavior of the main branch and discuss why a guard might not be necessary if the same key is reused intentionally across actions. It's important to note that the map is designed as one key leading to one action. I also want to inspect whether the main branch overwrites from flat effective keybindings. Inherited behavior might also come into play, especially since the object iteration order in the app loop can overwrite previous settings if the same normalized key appears later. Let's create this explanation without using tools.

content.text:
You may not need one.

Why reviewer called it out
- in `buildBuiltinKeybindings`, we build:
  - `Record<KeyId, BuiltInKeyBinding>`
- that means one key can map to only one built-in entry in that conflict map
- if two built-in actions use the same key, the later write wins

That is what they mean by “silent”:
- no warning
- no assertion
- just overwrite

Why it still works today
- because that was already true before this change
- the old code also built a flat map:
  - one normalized key -> one built-in action
- if multiple built-in actions shared a key, the last one in iteration order won there too

How it works on main branch
Before this change, main branch did roughly this:

```ts
for (const [action, keys] of Object.entries(effectiveKeybindings)) {
  for (const key of keyList) {
    builtinKeybindings[normalizedKey] = {
      action,
      restrictOverride,
    };
  }
}
```

So on main too:
- `enter` could correspond to both:
  - `submit`
  - `selectConfirm`
- whichever action was iterated last became the one stored in the conflict map

Same for:
- `ctrl+c`
- `ctrl+d`
- `ctrl+o`
- etc

So the overwrite behavior is not introduced by this patch.
It already existed.

What changed with this patch
- now we do two loops:
  - editor metadata
  - app metadata
- so overwrite behavior is a bit more explicit structurally
- but semantically, it is still the same old model:
  - one key -> one built-in conflict entry

Why reviewer mentioned `expandTools`
Because `expandTools` currently exists in both:
- editor keybindings
- app keybindings

Both use:
- `ctrl+o`
- same scope
- same effective meaning

So with the new two-loop structure:
- editor loop writes `ctrl+o`
- app loop writes `ctrl+o` again
- app entry wins
- no visible issue, since both represent the same same-named action

So do we need a guard?
Not strictly, if you are okay with the existing semantics:
- the conflict checker is intentionally approximate
- one key resolves to one built-in owner
- duplicates are already normal in the system

When a guard would help
A guard is useful only if you want to catch accidental cases like:
- same key in both maps
- but different scope than expected
- or different action wins unexpectedly
- or future refactors introduce ambiguous ownership

So it is more of a maintainability/debugging aid than a correctness fix for the current patch.

My opinion
- not required for this change
- current behavior is consistent with main
- if you want to keep this first pass simple, I would not add the guard now

If later you want stricter behavior, there are two better paths:
1. add a dev-only assertion for suspicious duplicates
2. change the conflict map to allow multiple built-in bindings per key and decide conflicts based on all matching entries

But that second one is a larger design change.

Short answer
- reviewer is pointing out an existing “one key, last entry wins” behavior
- your patch did not fundamentally create it
- main branch already behaves that way
- a guard is optional, not necessary, for this first pass

input.title: feat(keybindings): scope built-in shortcut conflicts
input.body:
## Summary
- introduce explicit scopes for built-in keybindings in the TUI keybinding model
- derive default app/editor keybinding maps from scope-aware metadata
- limit extension shortcut conflict detection to built-in shortcuts active in `global` and `editor` contexts
- update changelogs for `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent` and `@mariozechner/pi-tui`

## Problem
Extension shortcut conflict warnings were built from the full flattened built-in keybinding set. That produced false positives for built-in shortcuts that only apply in specialized contexts like the session picker, tree picker, or selection UI, even though those shortcuts are not active during normal editor input.

## Changes
### `packages/tui`
- add `KeybindingScope`
- add scope-aware metadata for built-in editor and app keybindings
- derive `DEFAULT_EDITOR_KEYBINDINGS` and `DEFAULT_APP_KEYBINDINGS` from metadata
- export the new scope types/metadata from the package index

Current first-pass scopes:
- `global`
- `editor`
- `selection`
- `sessionPicker`
- `treePicker`

### `packages/coding-agent`
- update built-in keybinding collection used for extension conflict detection
- include only built-ins in `global` or `editor` scopes when checking extension shortcut conflicts
- keep existing reserved-action behavior unchanged
- clarify conflict warning text to mention the built-in action scope

## Result
This removes false-positive extension shortcut warnings for built-in bindings that are only active outside normal editor/global contexts, while keeping real conflicts visible.

Manual validation done with a temporary one-file extension:
- `ctrl+b` still warns because it conflicts with an editor-scoped built-in shortcut
- `ctrl+r` does not warn when the only built-in use is in a non-editor/non-global scope
- extension shortcuts still fire as expected

## Notes
- this is a first-pass scoped model: one scope per built-in binding, no predicate/`when` system yet
- duplicate normalized built-in keys still use existing last-write-wins behavior; this PR does not change that behavior

Prepare a pull request for the current branch against `badlogic/pi-mono`. Follow these steps in order.

## 1. Read AGENTS.md

Read `AGENTS.md` in the project root. Pay attention to:
- Changelog format rules (sections, attribution, `[Unreleased]` placement)
- Commit message format (conventional commits)

## 2. Get next PR number

Call `pr_next_number` to determine the next available issue/PR number. You need this for the changelog entries.

## 3. Identify affected packages

Run `git diff upstream/main --name-only` to see changed files. Determine which `packages/*/CHANGELOG.md` files need entries based on the paths.

## 4. Update changelogs

For each affected package:
1. Read the full `packages/<pkg>/CHANGELOG.md`
2. Find the `## [Unreleased]` section

4. Append an entry using external contributor format:

```
Description ([#NNN](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono/pull/NNN) by [@aliou](https://github.com/aliou))
```

Where `NNN` is the number from step 2. Do not create duplicate subsections. Append to existing ones.

## 5. Commit changelog update

Stage the changed `CHANGELOG.md` files. Then:

- If the changes are already committed in a single commit, amend that commit with the changelog files (`git commit --amend --no-edit`).
- If the changes are not yet committed, commit everything together (changes + changelog) in one commit.
- If there are multiple commits because the changes are not atomic enough to be a single commit, create a separate changelog commit:
  ```
  docs: update changelog for PR #NNN
  ```

## 6. Open PR

Call `pr_open` with:
- `title`: a concise summary of all changes (conventional commit style, e.g. `fix(ai): handle empty responses`)
- `body`: a detailed markdown summary of what changed and why, organized by package/logical section. Include file paths and behavioral changes.

The tool handles pushing, body formatting, and opening the PR in the browser. Do not submit the PR -- it will be opened for review.

input.title: feat(keybindings): scope built-in shortcut conflicts
input.body:
## Summary

This changes built-in shortcut conflict detection so extensions only conflict with built-in shortcuts that are active in normal global/editor contexts.

## Problem

Extension shortcut conflict detection was built from the full built-in keybinding set. That caused false-positive warnings for built-in shortcuts that only apply in narrower contexts such as session picker, tree picker, or selection UIs.

In practice, an extension shortcut could warn even when the built-in shortcut was not active in the editor context where the extension shortcut is actually used.

## packages/tui

### `packages/tui/src/keybindings.ts`
- Added `KeybindingScope` with these first-pass scopes:
  - `global`
  - `editor`
  - `selection`
  - `sessionPicker`
  - `treePicker`
- Added `DEFAULT_EDITOR_KEYBINDING_METADATA` with `{ keys, scope }` metadata per built-in editor action.
- Derived `DEFAULT_EDITOR_KEYBINDINGS` from metadata instead of maintaining a separate default map.
- Scoped built-in editor actions such as:
  - normal editor editing/navigation actions to `editor`
  - autocomplete/selection actions to `selection`
  - session picker actions to `sessionPicker`
  - tree navigation actions to `treePicker`

### `packages/tui/src/index.ts`
- Exported the new scope type and metadata needed by downstream packages.

## packages/coding-agent

### `packages/coding-agent/src/core/keybindings.ts`
- Added `DEFAULT_APP_KEYBINDING_METADATA` with `{ keys, scope }` for app-level actions.
- Derived `DEFAULT_APP_KEYBINDINGS` from that metadata.
- Scoped app actions so conflict detection can distinguish global/editor shortcuts from picker-specific ones.

### `packages/coding-agent/src/core/extensions/runner.ts`
- Updated built-in shortcut collection for extension conflict detection.
- Conflict checking now includes only built-in shortcuts in `global` and `editor` scopes.
- Kept existing reserved-action behavior intact via `RESERVED_ACTIONS_FOR_EXTENSION_CONFLICTS`.
- Warning text now includes the built-in action and scope when an extension overrides a non-reserved built-in shortcut.

## Behavior change

Before:
- extension shortcuts could warn against built-ins that are only active in session-picker/tree-picker/selection contexts

After:
- extension shortcuts are checked only against built-ins active in `global` or `editor` scope
- reserved built-ins still block extension overrides as before
- non-reserved global/editor built-ins still warn, but no longer produce false positives from unrelated scopes

## Files changed

- `packages/tui/src/keybindings.ts`
- `packages/tui/src/index.ts`
- `packages/tui/CHANGELOG.md`
- `packages/coding-agent/src/core/keybindings.ts`
- `packages/coding-agent/src/core/extensions/runner.ts`
- `packages/coding-agent/CHANGELOG.md`

## Validation

Manual validation with a temporary extension confirmed:
- `ctrl+b` still warns because it overlaps an editor-scope built-in shortcut
- `ctrl+r` no longer warns when the only overlapping built-in action is session-picker scoped
- both shortcuts still fire at runtime in the expected contexts

## Notes

This is a first-pass scoped keybinding model.

It intentionally does not add a more complex override policy system or `when`-style predicates. It also preserves the existing last-write-wins behavior when multiple built-ins normalize to the same key in the flat conflict map.
